past_medical_history: Migraine without aura, diagnosed at age 19, infrequent episodes managed with sumatriptan as needed
Iron deficiency anemia treated with oral supplementation, resolved 2 years ago
family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes mellitus, age 62
Maternal uncle died of colon cancer at age 55
No family history of brain tumors or neurological disorders

social_history.alcohol: Occasional social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives with partner in an apartment
social_history.exercise: Yoga 3 times per week, occasional running

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Attentive, performs serial sevens without error. Language fluent with no dysarthria or aphasia. Follows three-step commands. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. Fund of knowledge intact. Judgment and insight preserved.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light, 3 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Fundoscopic exam reveals early papilledema bilaterally with blurred disc margins. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch and pinprick bilaterally. No facial asymmetry. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: 5/5 throughout. Right lower extremity: 5/5 throughout. Left upper extremity: 4/5 deltoid, 4+/5 biceps, 4+/5 triceps, 4/5 grip, 4/5 finger abduction. Left lower extremity: 4+/5 hip flexion, 5/5 knee extension, 4+/5 ankle dorsiflexion, 5/5 plantar flexion. Pronator drift present on the left. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact bilaterally in all extremities. Proprioception intact at the great toes bilaterally. Vibration sense intact at the ankles bilaterally. No cortical sensory findings.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right upper extremity 2+, right lower extremity 2+. Left upper extremity 3+, left lower extremity 3+. Plantar response extensor (Babinski positive) on the left, flexor on the right.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose mildly dysmetric on the left, intact on the right. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements slightly slowed on the left.
neurological_exam.gait: Ambulates independently with mild left-sided circumduction. Reduced left arm swing. No Romberg sign. Tandem gait slightly impaired.
neurological_exam.additional: No meningismus. Early papilledema noted bilaterally on fundoscopy. One witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure in the emergency department lasting approximately two minutes, preceded by left arm clonic jerking.

patient.chief_complaint: Worsening headaches, progressive left-sided weakness, and a first-time seizure
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Priya Nair is a 31-year-old right-handed woman with a history of infrequent migraines who presents to the emergency department after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure. She reports that over the past six weeks, she has been experiencing headaches that are qualitatively different from her usual migraines: they are constant, dull, pressure-like, and worst upon awakening in the morning, sometimes accompanied by nausea. Sumatriptan, which previously controlled her migraines effectively, has had no effect on these new headaches. Over the past three weeks, she noticed progressive difficulty with her left hand, initially manifesting as clumsiness when typing on her keyboard and difficulty manipulating small objects. Her partner confirms that the left-hand weakness has been worsening steadily and that she has been dropping items more frequently. She also describes feeling more fatigued than usual and has had two episodes of nausea with vomiting over the past week, occurring in the morning without clear provocation. She denies any visual changes, speech difficulties, sensory loss, bowel or bladder dysfunction, fever, weight loss, night sweats, or recent infections. She has no history of malignancy, no prior seizures, and no recent travel. This morning, while sitting at her desk, she experienced rhythmic jerking of her left arm followed by a generalized convulsion witnessed by her partner, who called emergency services. She was postictal and drowsy for approximately twenty minutes. A non-contrast CT scan obtained in the emergency department demonstrated a large hyperdense mass in the right frontoparietal region with surrounding hypodensity and approximately 9 mm of midline shift to the left. She was started on intravenous dexamethasone and levetiracetam and admitted to the neurology service for further evaluation.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, well-formed over the left hemisphere, attenuated over the right
background.symmetry: Marked asymmetry with attenuation and continuous slowing over the right frontoparietal region
background.reactivity: PDR attenuates with eye opening on the left; attenuated background over the right shows minimal reactivity

findings.morphology: Focal amplitude asymmetry with higher amplitudes over the right frontocentral area
Sharp-wave complexes with aftergoing slow waves, quasi-periodic

artifacts.impact: Mild, does not obscure cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to concern for elevated intracranial pressure

eeg.impression: Background asymmetry with significant attenuation over the right hemisphere. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Hyperventilation not performed due to clinical concern for elevated intracranial pressure. Recording duration 30 minutes.

findings.type: Heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis
findings.location: Right frontoparietal lobe, centered in the precentral gyrus and superior frontal gyrus, extending into the subcortical white matter and corona radiata

signal_characteristics.T1: Heterogeneously hypointense with foci of intrinsic T1 hyperintensity suggesting hemorrhage or proteinaceous debris
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneous with central T2 hyperintensity indicating necrosis and peripheral intermediate signal
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Extensive perilesional FLAIR hyperintensity involving the right frontal and parietal white matter, extending into the ipsilateral corona radiata and approaching the corpus callosum
signal_characteristics.DWI: Restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim indicating high cellularity; central necrotic region shows facilitated diffusion
signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: Thick, irregular ring enhancement with nodular enhancing components along the superolateral and posterior margins; central non-enhancing necrotic core

findings.mass_effect: Effacement of the right frontal horn and body of the lateral ventricle. Approximately 9 mm leftward midline shift of the septum pellucidum. Compression of the right caudate head and anterior limb of the internal capsule. Sulcal effacement over the right frontoparietal convexity. Early subfalcine herniation.
findings.borders: Irregular and infiltrative margins with indistinct interface with adjacent brain parenchyma, particularly along the medial and posterior aspects

findings.location: Right frontoparietal white matter extending into the corona radiata, centrum semiovale, and approaching the splenium of the corpus callosum

signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion in edematous regions

findings.mass_effect: Contributing to overall mass effect and midline shift
findings.borders: Finger-like extensions along white matter tracts

volumetrics.lesion_volume: Approximately 71 cm³ including enhancing and necrotic components
volumetrics.edema_volume: Approximately 95 cm³ of perilesional FLAIR signal abnormality

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions elsewhere in the brain parenchyma
No hydrocephalus despite significant midline shift
Major dural venous sinuses are patent on post-contrast sequences
Mild mucosal thickening in the right maxillary sinus, likely incidental
mri.impression: Large heterogeneously enhancing right frontoparietal mass with central necrosis, thick irregular ring enhancement, extensive surrounding vasogenic edema, and 9 mm of leftward midline shift with early subfalcine herniation. Restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim suggests high cellularity. No evidence of additional intracranial lesions or leptomeningeal spread. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 88 bpm. No acute ischemic changes. Normal intervals and axis. Unremarkable ECG.

findings.location: Enhancing rim of the right frontoparietal mass, particularly along the superolateral and posterior margins

signal_characteristics.Ktrans: Elevated, indicating blood-brain barrier disruption and neoangiogenesis

additional_observations: Hot spot of rCBV along the posterior enhancing rim (ratio 4.1), likely representing the highest-grade component and optimal biopsy target
Perilesional edema demonstrates mildly elevated rCBV (ratio 1.3) compared to contralateral normal-appearing white matter, suggesting infiltrative tumor beyond the enhancing margin
output.impression: MR perfusion demonstrates markedly elevated rCBV (ratio 4.1) in the enhancing rim of the right frontoparietal mass, strongly supporting a high-grade glial neoplasm. Central necrotic core shows expected low perfusion. Perilesional perfusion abnormalities suggest infiltrative tumor extending beyond the contrast-enhancing boundary. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.type: Abnormal metabolite ratios consistent with high-grade neoplasm
findings.location: Enhancing component of right frontoparietal mass

signal_characteristics.lipid_lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks, indicating necrosis and anaerobic metabolism

findings.location: FLAIR-hyperintense region adjacent to enhancing mass

additional_observations: Perilesional spectral changes suggest infiltrative tumor beyond the enhancing margin
output.impression: Findings support glioblastoma. Clinical correlation recommended.

CT_Chest_Abdomen_Pelvis.value: Clear, no masses, nodules, or lymphadenopathy
Normal size and enhancement bilaterally, no masses or stones
No suspicious masses or lymphadenopathy. Small physiologic ovarian cyst on the left, 1.5 cm
No suspicious lytic or blastic lesions

Histopathology.value: Highly cellular glial neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (15 mitoses per 10 HPF), florid microvascular proliferation with glomeruloid bodies, and extensive palisading and pseudopalisading necrosis

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Microscopic description Highly cellular glial neoplasm with marked nuclear atypia, brisk mitotic activity (15 mitoses per 10 HPF), florid microvascular proliferation with glomeruloid bodies, and extensive palisading and pseudopalisading necrosis N/A (H), Ki-67 proliferation index 40% % (H), p53 immunostain Focally positive (30% of tumor cells) N/A (H), IDH1/IDH2 mutation Wild-type (negative by immunohistochemistry and sequencing) N/A (H), MGMT promoter methylation Methylated N/A (H), TERT promoter mutation Present N/A (H), +7/-10 chromosome Present N/A (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Microscopic description: Highly cellular glial neoplasm with microvascular proliferation and palisading necrosis (H)

signal_characteristics.left_hand_activation: BOLD activation identified in the right precentral gyrus, significantly displaced posteriorly and inferiorly by the mass. The activation focus for left hand motor function is located approximately 5 mm from the inferoposterior enhancing margin of the tumor.
signal_characteristics.right_hand_activation: Normal BOLD activation in the left precentral gyrus, anatomically preserved
signal_characteristics.foot_activation: Bilateral paracentral lobule activation, right-sided activation mildly displaced medially by the mass

findings.mass_effect: The tumor extensively involves and displaces the right primary motor cortex. The hand knob region is markedly distorted and partially infiltrated.

signal_characteristics.Broca_area: Normal BOLD activation in the left inferior frontal gyrus
signal_characteristics.Wernicke_area: Normal BOLD activation in the left posterior superior temporal gyrus

additional_observations: DTI tractography demonstrates the right corticospinal tract is displaced medially and posteriorly; the tract appears stretched and thinned but not interrupted
Superior longitudinal fasciculus on the right is displaced inferiorly
No BOLD activation identified within the enhancing tumor mass
output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates that the right frontoparietal mass extensively displaces the primary motor cortex. Language function is left-lateralized and not at direct surgical risk. DTI shows displacement and thinning of the right corticospinal tract. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: glioblastoma young adults prognosis IDH-wildtype treatment

results.title: Glioblastoma in young adults: a distinct molecular and clinical entity
results.key_finding: Young adult GBM patients (age < 40) have distinct molecular profiles with higher rates of IDH mutation, but IDH-wildtype GBM in young adults carries a similarly poor prognosis as in older patients

results.title: Radiotherapy plus concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide for glioblastoma
results.authors: Stupp R, Mason WP, van den Bent MJ, et al.
results.key_finding: The Stupp protocol (concurrent chemoradiation followed by adjuvant temozolomide) remains the standard of care for newly diagnosed glioblastoma regardless of age

results.title: MGMT gene silencing and benefit from temozolomide in glioblastoma
results.authors: Hegi ME, Diserens AC, Gorlia T, et al.
results.key_finding: MGMT promoter methylation is a strong predictive biomarker for benefit from temozolomide; methylated patients have significantly improved survival with temozolomide-based therapy

results.title: Tumor-treating fields plus maintenance temozolomide for glioblastoma
results.authors: Stupp R, Taillibert S, Kanner A, et al.
results.key_finding: Addition of tumor-treating fields (TTFields) to maintenance temozolomide improved median overall survival to 20.9 months in newly diagnosed glioblastoma

output.summary: Glioblastoma in young adults (age < 40) is uncommon and warrants careful molecular characterization, as many tumors in this age group are IDH-mutant and carry a better prognosis. IDH-wildtype GBM in young adults, however, behaves similarly to GBM in older patients and carries a poor prognosis. Standard treatment is maximal safe resection followed by the Stupp protocol (concurrent radiation 60 Gy with temozolomide, then adjuvant temozolomide). MGMT promoter methylation predicts improved response to temozolomide. TTFields should be discussed as an adjunct. Given the young age, clinical trial enrollment should be strongly considered.

output.proposed: Levetiracetam 500 mg IV twice daily + Dexamethasone 4 mg IV every 6 hours
interactions: Oral contraceptive + Dexamethasone: Corticosteroids may alter estrogen metabolism; clinical significance is low with short-term use but monitor for breakthrough bleeding
Dexamethasone + Vitamin D: Corticosteroids can interfere with vitamin D metabolism and calcium absorption; consider increasing vitamin D supplementation
warnings: Levetiracetam: Monitor for psychiatric side effects (irritability, depression, psychosis), especially in the perioperative period
Dexamethasone: Taper to the lowest effective dose as soon as clinically feasible to minimize steroid side effects
Monitor blood glucose while on dexamethasone; steroid-induced hyperglycemia can occur even in non-diabetic patients
Dexamethasone: Consider GI prophylaxis with proton pump inhibitor
Discontinue oral contraceptive prior to surgery due to thrombotic risk in the perioperative period
output.formulary_status: Both medications on formulary without restrictions
alternatives: Lacosamide as alternative antiepileptic if levetiracetam is not tolerated
Methylprednisolone as alternative corticosteroid, though dexamethasone is preferred in neuro-oncology

differential.key_features: Less prominent necrosis and microvascular proliferation expected in grade III; the degree of central necrosis and thick ring enhancement in this case strongly favor grade IV
Single lesion in a young woman with no known primary malignancy and negative systemic staging CT; infiltrative margins more consistent with primary brain tumor
Immunocompetent young patient; prominent necrosis and heterogeneous ring enhancement are atypical for lymphoma
No fever, normal inflammatory markers, no history of infection or immunocompromise; restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim rather than centrally argues against abscess
No history of multiple sclerosis; closed ring enhancement with significant necrosis and mass effect argue against demyelination; disproportionate mass effect for a demyelinating lesion

optimal_actions.action: Obtain urgent CT head to assess for mass lesion and evaluate for herniation risk
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large right frontoparietal hyperdense mass with surrounding edema and midline shift

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dexamethasone 10 mg IV loading dose followed by 4 mg IV every 6 hours to reduce peritumoral edema and prevent herniation
Start levetiracetam 500 mg IV twice daily for seizure management given presenting seizure and PLEDs on EEG
Order MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast to characterize the lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis, ring enhancement, surrounding edema, and midline shift consistent with high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Order routine EEG to characterize epileptiform activity and guide antiepileptic management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right frontoparietal focal slowing with PLEDs indicating high seizure risk

optimal_actions.action: Order baseline labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, and liver function tests
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs providing adequate baseline for surgical intervention and chemotherapy

optimal_actions.action: Request MR perfusion imaging to assess tumor vascularity and grade
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV in enhancing rim consistent with high-grade tumor

optimal_actions.action: Request MR spectroscopy to evaluate metabolite profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated choline, reduced NAA, lipid/lactate peaks consistent with high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to rule out primary malignancy elsewhere (metastasis workup)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of primary extracranial malignancy

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurosurgery for maximal safe resection or stereotactic biopsy
Request functional MRI for pre-surgical motor and language mapping given tumor proximity to motor cortex
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Motor cortex displaced by tumor, language lateralized to left hemisphere

optimal_actions.action: Following tissue diagnosis, plan Stupp protocol: concurrent chemoradiation (60 Gy + temozolomide) followed by adjuvant temozolomide
Discontinue oral contraceptive prior to surgery due to perioperative thrombotic risk
Discuss tumor-treating fields (TTFields) as adjunct to maintenance temozolomide

critical_actions: Initiate dexamethasone to reduce peritumoral edema and prevent herniation given 9 mm midline shift
Start antiepileptic medication (levetiracetam) given presenting seizure and PLEDs on EEG
Obtain MRI brain with contrast to characterize the mass
Rule out metastatic disease with systemic imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis)
Obtain tissue diagnosis via neurosurgical biopsy or resection with molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q)
Plan for Stupp protocol (concurrent chemoradiation followed by adjuvant temozolomide) after tissue confirmation
contraindicated_actions: Lumbar puncture in the setting of a large mass with 9 mm midline shift and early subfalcine herniation (herniation risk)
Empiric whole-brain radiation without tissue diagnosis
Delaying corticosteroids in a patient with significant mass effect and early herniation signs
Starting temozolomide prior to tissue confirmation of glioblastoma diagnosis
Administering anticoagulation without clear indication given upcoming surgery
Continuing oral contraceptive perioperatively (increased thrombotic risk)
key_reasoning_points: Progressive headaches that are qualitatively different from known migraines, worst in the morning with nausea and vomiting, suggest elevated intracranial pressure and warrant urgent imaging
New progressive focal motor deficit (left hand weakness) with a first-time seizure in a young adult is a classic presentation of a brain tumor
CT showing a large mass with significant midline shift mandates immediate dexamethasone and neurosurgical consultation
MRI features of heterogeneous ring enhancement with central necrosis, thick irregular rim, and surrounding vasogenic edema are characteristic of glioblastoma
Although GBM is uncommon in young adults and IDH-mutant tumors are more frequent in this age group, IDH-wildtype GBM can occur at any age and carries similarly poor prognosis
Metastasis workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) is necessary even when imaging strongly suggests primary brain tumor to exclude extracranial malignancy
Tissue diagnosis with molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, TERT, 1p/19q) is essential before initiating definitive therapy per WHO 2021 classification
GFAP positivity on biopsy confirms astrocytic lineage, consistent with the source case which also demonstrated GFAP-immunoreactive astrocytic tumor
EEG showing PLEDs indicates high seizure risk and supports aggressive antiepileptic management

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic ring-enhancing mass with necrosis and midline shift on imaging. Clear progressive focal deficits and first-time seizure in a young woman. Minimal confounders. Key diagnostic tools confirm diagnosis with high confidence.
